schools.summary: The exhortation following the rebuke of their dullness (5:11-14): 'leaving (aphentes — leaving behind, moving on from, not abandoning) the principles (the elementary teaching, the ABCs, the 'word of the beginning') of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on (be borne on) unto perfection (teleioteta — maturity, full growth)' — believers to advance from the rudiments to mature understanding; 'not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works (turning from sinful or lifeless deeds) and of faith toward God' — the first two of six foundational topics, not to be laid again repeatedly but built upon (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: leaving the principles of the doctrine of Christ, let us go on unto perfection
not laying again the foundation of repentance from dead works, and of faith toward God
the call to leave the first principles and go on to maturity
building on the foundation

schools.summary: The four further foundational topics (with repentance and faith, v.1, making six): 'the doctrine of baptisms (baptismon — washings, instruction about the various ceremonial washings and Christian baptism), and of laying on of hands (the rite by which gifts, blessing, or office were conferred), and of resurrection of the dead (the raising of the body), and of eternal judgment (the final, everlasting judgment)' — these six items forming the basic catechism, the rudiments of Christian instruction from which the readers are to advance to maturity (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Of the doctrine of baptisms, and of laying on of hands, and of resurrection of the dead, and of eternal judgment
the remaining foundational doctrines
the basic catechism
the rudiments to be built upon

schools.summary: The writer's resolve: 'and this will we do (we will go on to maturity, advance to the higher teaching), if God permit (if God allows, grants it)' — purposing to lead them on to the deeper truths (the priesthood of Christ after Melchizedek), not lingering at the foundation; 'if God permit' recognizing that progress in spiritual understanding is God's gift, the teacher's leading and the hearers' growth depending on his enabling grace (and perhaps hinting that, if they are hardened, vv.4-6, the advance may not be possible) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: And this will we do, if God permit
the resolve to advance, if God permit
leading them on to maturity
progress depending on God's grace

schools.summary: The description piles up the marks of Christian experience: 'once (hapax — once for all) enlightened (photisthentas — illumined by the gospel, the word used by the early church of baptism and conversion), and have tasted of the heavenly gift (experienced salvation, the forgiveness of sins, the gift from heaven), and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost (shared the Spirit's renewing influences)' — the richest language of grace. Barnes argues strongly these phrases describe true Christians, cannot properly apply to any but the regenerate, and the warning is to keep true believers from apostasy (the great mass of readers would so take it, unless a theory required otherwise).

Calvin and Gill: the reprobate may receive 'some taste' of God's grace — a temporary illumination, sparks of light, a 'temporal faith' (Mark 4:17) that does not strike deep root and afterwards vanishes — without being truly regenerate; God favours none but the elect with the Spirit of regeneration, and the elect (given to Christ to keep, John 10:28) are beyond the danger of finally falling away. So these may be enlightened professors, not the truly born again.

Clarke and Wesley: these are real spiritual experiences and marks of genuine grace — enlightenment with the love of God in Christ, tasting the heavenly gift (remission of sins, sweeter than honey), partaking of the Holy Ghost (the witness and fruit of the Spirit); the persons are truly partakers of salvation, from which (they hold) it is possible to fall.

themes: it is impossible for those who were once enlightened, and have tasted of the heavenly gift, and were made partakers of the Holy Ghost
the description of those who experienced the great blessings
true Christians or temporary professors
the marks of Christian experience

schools.summary: The continuation of the description: 'and have tasted the good word of God (experienced the excellence and sweetness of God's word, the truth of the gospel, relished and delighted in by the renewed heart) and the powers of the world to come (dynameis mellontos aionos — the energies of the coming age, the Messianic age)' — the 'powers of the world to come' read either as the miraculous powers and gifts that attended the gospel age (Clarke: the miracles confirming the gospel) or as the foretastes of heavenly blessedness, the energies of the coming age experienced now (Wesley, Barnes: the hope full of immortality) — Barnes, Robertson, Clarke.

themes: have tasted the good word of God, and the powers of the world to come
the further blessings tasted
the good word and the powers of the coming age
foretastes of heavenly glory

schools.summary: Barnes holds the persons are true Christians, but the falling-away is hypothetical: were a true Christian to apostatize, it would be impossible to renew him — yet in fact none ever do (John 10:28; Romans 8:38-39; 1 John 2:19), the warning being the very means God uses to preserve them (as knowing a precipice means certain death keeps men from falling). 'Impossible (adynaton)' is absolute, not merely difficult; should one apostatize his case is hopeless, for he rejects the only plan of salvation after experiencing it. Ellicott takes the impossibility to relate to human action — impossible for the teacher to renew such by the ordinary means of God's kingdom (v.3, 'if God permit'); the 'crucifying afresh' (anastaurountas) and 'putting to open shame' describe a continuing state of deliberate hostility, returning to the Jews' rejection of Christ without the palliation of ignorance.

Clarke and Wesley take it as a real and dreadful possibility. Wesley: 'here is not a supposition, but a plain relation of fact' — the case of 'wilful total apostates' who have cast away both the power and form of godliness, lost faith, hope, and love, and wilfully (10:26) renounced Christ; of these it is impossible to renew them again, though they were renewed once. Clarke: the inserted 'if' (from Beza) is a Calvinist bias with no MS authority — the Greek is 'having fallen away'; there is 'a fearful possibility of falling away from the grace of God.' The apostasy in view is the total rejection of the gospel and blasphemy of the Saviour, joining the Jews who crucified him.

Calvin and Gill: the falling away is a total defection from the gospel — not theft or adultery but an entire renouncing of God's grace, the sin against the Holy Spirit, of which no truly regenerate person is guilty. God deprives none of his grace so as to leave the elect none remaining; the apostates here were reprobates who had only a temporary taste (v.4). Gill: this is not supposed of true believers, who are held by a threefold cord (Father, Son, and Spirit) and cannot fall finally; when any rise after falling, it proves they had not truly apostatized. Calvin also notes some read it 'impossible for us (teachers) to renew them,' the impossibility being on the human side.

debates.question: Can those described (6:4-5) truly fall away and be lost, and who are they?

sides.position: True Christians, but the case is hypothetical — God preserves the regenerate so it never actually happens; 'impossible' is absolute
True believers who really can and do wholly, wilfully apostatize and become irrecoverable (Arminian)
Not the truly regenerate but reprobates/professors with a temporary taste of grace; the elect never finally fall (Reformed)

themes: If they shall fall away, to renew them again unto repentance
seeing they crucify to themselves the Son of God afresh, and put him to an open shame
the impossibility of renewing the apostate
the great perseverance crux

schools.summary: The illustration of the warning: 'for the earth which drinketh in (absorbs) the rain that cometh oft upon it, and bringeth forth herbs (useful vegetation) meet (fit, profitable) for them by whom it is dressed (cultivated), receiveth blessing from God' — ground that, watered by frequent rain, yields a useful crop for its cultivators is blessed, fruitful, approved; the 'rain' being the repeated teaching and grace the readers had received (vv.4-5), the fruitful ground picturing those who use it well and are blessed (the believer who brings forth the fruit of holiness), over against the barren ground (v.8) that pictures the apostate (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

themes: the earth which drinketh in the rain that cometh oft upon it, and bringeth forth herbs meet for them by whom it is dressed
receiveth blessing from God
the fruitful ground that receives blessing
the believer who bears the fruit of grace

schools.summary: The contrast (the dark side of the parable): 'but that which beareth thorns and briers (worthless, harmful growth, despite the same rain) is rejected (adokimos — disapproved, reprobate, cast aside as useless), and is nigh unto cursing (close to being cursed, on the verge of the curse); whose end is to be burned (the burning of the worthless field, the figure of destruction)' — ground that, though watered, yields only thorns being worthless and near to condemnation; the figure of the apostate who, having received the same grace, brings forth no fruit but evil, whose end is destruction (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: that which beareth thorns and briers is rejected, and is nigh unto cursing; whose end is to be burned
the barren ground that is rejected and burned
the fruitless apostate near the curse
whose end is destruction

schools.summary: The change from warning to encouragement: 'but, beloved (a term of affection, used only here in the epistle), we are persuaded (confident, convinced) better things of you, and things that accompany (belong to, are connected with) salvation' — after the severe warning, the writer tenderly assuring them he expects better of them than apostasy, persuaded that they possess the marks and fruits of genuine salvation, not the barren thorns of v.8; 'though we thus speak' — the warning given not because he despaired of them but to guard them, severe warning and warm confidence going together (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: beloved, we are persuaded better things of you, and things that accompany salvation
though we thus speak
the writer's better hope for the readers
warning the loved from confidence not despair

schools.summary: The reason for his better hope: 'for God is not unrighteous (it would be contrary to his justice and faithfulness) to forget your work and labour of love, which ye have shewed toward his name (for God's sake, out of love to him), in that ye have ministered (served, helped) to the saints, and do minister' — God not overlooking or forgetting their faithful service, his justice guaranteeing that genuine works of love are remembered; their past and continuing ministry to fellow-believers being evidence of genuine grace, a fruit accompanying salvation (v.9) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry.

themes: God is not unrighteous to forget your work and labour of love, which ye have shewed toward his name
in that ye have ministered to the saints, and do minister
God's faithful remembrance of their work and love
the fruits that accompany salvation

schools.summary: The writer's earnest wish: 'and we desire (earnestly wish) that every one of you (each individually) do shew the same diligence (spoude — earnestness, zeal)' — the same diligence they showed in love (v.10) now to be shown in pressing on, the exhortation personal to each; 'to the full assurance (plerophoria — full conviction, certainty) of hope' — that their diligence may issue in a firm, settled assurance of their hope of salvation (not doubt but full confidence); 'unto the end' — continuing to the close of life, perseverance to the end being the mark (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: we desire that every one of you do shew the same diligence to the full assurance of hope unto the end
the desire that each show diligence to full assurance
perseverance to the end
diligence issuing in settled hope

schools.summary: The exhortation: 'that ye be not slothful (nothroi — sluggish, dull, the same word as 5:11), but followers (mimetai — imitators) of them who through faith (believing trust that lays hold) and patience (makrothymia — long-suffering, patient endurance that waits) inherit (obtain, come into possession of) the promises' — not sluggish but imitating the faithful who obtained the promises by faith and steadfast endurance together; the examples being the saints of old (Abraham chief among them, vv.13-15) whom the readers are to follow (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: that ye be not slothful, but followers of them who through faith and patience inherit the promises
the call to imitate the faithful
faith and patience inheriting the promises
not sluggish but imitating the saints

schools.summary: The example of Abraham, who obtained the promise: 'for when God made promise to Abraham (the promise of blessing and posterity, Genesis 22:16-17), because he could swear by no greater (there being none greater than himself to swear by), he sware by himself' — God confirming his promise with an oath, sworn by his own being; men swear by one greater (by God), but God, having none greater, swears by himself, by his own existence and faithfulness, the oath grounded in God himself, a condescension to human weakness to give the heirs the fullest assurance (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom).

themes: when God made promise to Abraham, because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself
God swearing by himself in his promise to Abraham
the divine oath grounded in God himself
God's condescension to assure the heirs

schools.summary: The words of the oath (Genesis 22:17): 'Surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee' — the Hebrew idiom of the doubled verb (blessing... bless, multiplying... multiply) expressing the certainty and abundance of the promise; God will surely and richly bless Abraham and multiply his seed (descendants as the stars and the sand), the great covenant promise fulfilled in his offspring and supremely in Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Surely blessing I will bless thee, and multiplying I will multiply thee
the sworn promise of blessing and multiplying
the doubled verb of certainty and abundance
the covenant promise to Abraham

schools.summary: The outcome: 'and so, after he had patiently endured (waited with patience, long-suffering), he obtained the promise' — Abraham, by patient waiting (for the birth of Isaac, and through many trials), came at last into possession of what God promised, faith and patience (v.12) exemplified; his patience under delay the pattern for the readers, and his obtaining the thing promised (the son, the beginning of the seed) a pledge of the full inheritance, God's faithfulness proved in his experience (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: And so, after he had patiently endured, he obtained the promise
Abraham obtaining the promise after patient endurance
the reward of patient faith
God's faithfulness proved

schools.summary: The general truth about human oaths: 'for men verily swear by the greater (by one greater than themselves, ultimately by God)' — in human affairs men confirm their word by appealing to a greater, calling God to witness; 'and an oath for confirmation (to confirm a statement) is to them an end (terminus, settlement) of all strife (dispute, contradiction)' — the oath settling disputes finally, ending controversy, the final guarantee among men; this human custom illustrating God's oath (vv.13-14), arguing from the lesser (human oaths) to the greater (God's absolute assurance) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: For men verily swear by the greater: and an oath for confirmation is to them an end of all strife
the human oath that ends all strife
the oath the final guarantee among men
illustrating God's oath

schools.summary: God's gracious intention: 'wherein God, willing more abundantly (more fully, beyond his bare word) to shew (demonstrate) unto the heirs of promise (not Abraham only but all believers, the spiritual seed who inherit the promise) the immutability (unchangeableness) of his counsel (his purpose, plan), confirmed it (interposed, guaranteed it) by an oath' — God wishing to make abundantly clear that his purpose cannot change, and for the heirs' assurance adding his oath to his unchangeable word, a double security; not that God's word needs confirming, but that he condescends to give the strongest possible ground of assurance (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: God, willing more abundantly to shew unto the heirs of promise the immutability of his counsel, confirmed it by an oath
God confirming his immutable counsel by an oath
the heirs of promise assured
a double security for full confidence

schools.summary: The two unchangeable things: 'that by two immutable things (the promise and the oath, God's word and his sworn confirmation), in which it was impossible for God to lie, we might have a strong (mighty, powerful) consolation (encouragement, comfort)' — two grounds, each absolutely sure (for God cannot lie), the believer's assurance resting on a double, unbreakable foundation, designed for his strong comfort; 'who have fled for refuge (the figure of the fugitive fleeing to the city of refuge or the altar) to lay hold upon (seize, grasp) the hope set before us' — believers, fleeing from danger, laying hold of the gospel hope as their refuge (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the imagery is of the soul fleeing for refuge, as to a sanctuary, and grasping the hope set before it; the believer, like the fugitive seizing the horns of the altar, lays hold of the gospel hope and finds in God's word and oath an unfailing refuge and strong consolation.

themes: by two immutable things, in which it was impossible for God to lie, we might have a strong consolation
who have fled for refuge to lay hold upon the hope set before us
the strong consolation grounded in God's word and oath
the soul fleeing to grasp the hope

schools.summary: The beautiful image: 'which hope we have as an anchor (ankyran) of the soul, both sure (asphale — firm, not slipping) and stedfast (bebaian — steady, immovable)' — the believer's hope the anchor of the soul, holding it firm and steady amid the storms of life (as an anchor holds a ship secure against wind and wave, so hope holds the soul firm against trials and temptations, anchored not adrift); 'and which entereth into that within the veil (the inner sanctuary, the Holy of Holies, here heaven itself)' — the anchor reaching up and within, into heaven, fastened to Christ behind the veil, the soul's hope anchored not in the shifting world but in the unseen, eternal sanctuary (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: unlike an earthly anchor that grips the seabed below, this anchor of hope reaches up into the Holy of Holies, where Christ has entered; the soul is fastened to him behind the veil, so that its hope is secured in heaven itself, immovable amid every trial.

themes: which hope we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and stedfast
which entereth into that within the veil
the hope as an anchor entering within the veil
the soul anchored to Christ in heaven

schools.summary: The striking word: 'whither (within the veil) the forerunner (prodromos — one who runs ahead, a pioneer who goes before to prepare the way for others) is for us entered, even Jesus' — Jesus having entered heaven as our forerunner; unlike the high priest who entered the earthly Holy of Holies alone (and whom none might follow), Jesus enters as a pioneer that his people may follow after him; 'made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec' (Psalm 110:4) — the great subject now to be unfolded (ch. 7), his priesthood eternal and after the Melchizedek order, not the Levitical (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril: under the law none might follow the high priest into the Holy of Holies; but Jesus enters as 'forerunner,' going before to prepare a place, that all his people may come after him into the sanctuary — the title unique to him, marking that his entrance opens the way for ours.

themes: whither the forerunner is for us entered, even Jesus
made an high priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec
Jesus the forerunner entered within the veil as high priest forever
the pioneer who opens the way for his people
